regulations.name: Online Safety Amendment (Social Media Minimum Age) Act 2024
regulations.summary: Australia's landmark legislation banning children under 16 from using social media platforms. Platforms must take reasonable steps to prevent underage users from holding accounts. Non-compliance penalties up to AUD 50 million.

obligations.description: Platforms must take reasonable steps, which may include age-assurance measures, to prevent children under 16 from holding age-restricted social media accounts
Covered social media services must prevent children under 16 from opening or holding accounts
Platforms must report on age assurance compliance measures annually

milestones.description: Bill introduced to Parliament as Online Safety Amendment Bill
Senate Environment and Communications References Committee report
Passed both houses of Parliament with bipartisan support
Received Royal Assent
12-month implementation period begins; effective date set for late 2025

regulations.name: Online Safety Act 2023
regulations.summary: UK's comprehensive online safety legislation requiring platforms to protect children from harmful content. Includes age assurance requirements for services likely to be accessed by children. Ofcom is the regulator.

obligations.description: Covered services must use highly effective age assurance where needed to prevent under-18 users from accessing pornography and other age-inappropriate harmful content
Age estimation is one of the methods services may use as part of highly effective age assurance for child-safety duties
Platforms must remove illegal content and content harmful to children
Platforms must conduct and publish annual children's safety risk assessments
Platforms must implement safety-by-design features for child users

milestones.description: Online Harms White Paper published
Online Safety Bill introduced to Parliament
Passed Parliament
Received Royal Assent
First wave of duties came into force
Ofcom begins enforcement of children's safety duties

regulations.name: Digital Services Act (DSA) — Age Assurance Provisions
regulations.summary: EU regulation requiring online platforms accessible to minors to protect minors' privacy, safety, and security. The DSA does not itself impose a general age-verification or parental-consent mandate, but it does impose minors-protection duties and additional systemic-risk obligations on very large platforms.

obligations.description: Online platforms accessible to minors must put in place appropriate and proportionate measures to ensure a high level of privacy, safety, and security of minors
Platforms may not present profiling-based advertising when they are aware with reasonable certainty that the recipient is a minor
Very large online platforms must assess systemic risks, including negative effects on the protection of minors, and document mitigation measures
Platforms should design recommender systems and interfaces in ways that support minors' privacy, safety, and security

milestones.description: European Commission proposed the DSA
Adopted by EU legislature
DSA entered into force
Full applicability date for all platforms

regulations.name: California Age-Appropriate Design Code Act (AB 2273)
regulations.summary: California law requiring businesses to design products likely to be accessed by children with privacy and safety in mind. Includes age estimation requirements and data protection obligations for minors.

obligations.description: A covered business must either estimate the age of child users with a reasonable level of certainty appropriate to the risks, or apply the child protections to all users
Configure privacy settings to highest level by default for minors
Prohibit use of children's data for profiling, targeted advertising, or dark patterns

milestones.description: AB 2273 introduced in California Assembly
Passed California Legislature
Signed by Governor Newsom
Federal court granted preliminary injunction in NetChoice v. Bonta

litigations.name: NetChoice v. Bonta
litigations.court: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit
litigations.description: NetChoice challenged the law as violating the First Amendment. District court granted preliminary injunction in September 2023; appeal pending.

regulations.name: Florida HB 3 — Social Media Age Verification Act
regulations.summary: Florida law prohibiting children under 14 from having social media accounts and requiring parental consent for 14-15 year olds. Platforms must terminate underage accounts and implement age verification.

obligations.description: Covered social media platforms must prohibit minors younger than 14 from becoming account holders and terminate those accounts
Minors aged 14 or 15 may become or remain account holders only with parental or guardian consent
Platforms must delete personal information associated with terminated minor accounts unless retention is legally required

milestones.description: HB 3 filed in Florida House
Passed Florida Legislature
Signed by Governor DeSantis
Law took effect
Enforcement by Florida AG begins

litigations.name: CCDH v. Uthmeier
litigations.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Florida
litigations.description: CCDH challenged the law on First Amendment grounds. Preliminary injunction denied.

regulations.name: Texas SCOPE Act (HB 18)
regulations.summary: Texas Securing Children Online through Parental Empowerment (SCOPE) Act requiring covered digital services to register user age, apply special duties toward known minors, provide parental tools, and prevent known minors' exposure to specified harmful content. It also separately requires 18+ age verification for services where more than one-third of content is harmful material or obscenity.

obligations.description: A covered digital service may not create an account unless the user has registered the user's age; users who register as under 18 are treated as known minors

For known minors, providers must limit data collection and use to what is reasonably necessary and may not allow purchases, share or sell data, collect precise geolocation, or serve targeted advertising

Providers must develop and implement a strategy to prevent known minors from being exposed to specified harmful material, including content promoting suicide, self-harm, eating disorders, substance abuse, stalking, bullying, harassment, grooming, trafficking, child sexual abuse material, or other sexual exploitation

milestones.description: HB 18 introduced in Texas House
Passed Texas Legislature
Signed by Governor Abbott
Law took effect after court ruling allowed enforcement

regulations.name: France Social Media Age Verification Law (SREN Bill)
regulations.summary: French law requiring social media platforms to verify user age and obtain parental consent for children under 15. Platforms must use certified age verification systems approved by ARCOM.

obligations.description: Social media platforms must verify user age using ARCOM-certified systems
Parental consent required for users under 15 to create social media accounts
Pornographic sites must use certified age verification to prevent minors from accessing content

milestones.description: SREN bill introduced to French Senate
Passed French Parliament
Promulgated by President Macron
Age verification provisions came into force

regulations.name: South Korea Youth Protection Act — Youth-Harmful Media Restrictions
regulations.summary: South Korea's youth-protection framework restricts minors' access to youth-harmful media and adult content. It is not a general social-media minimum-age law, and the former late-night gaming 'shutdown law' was repealed in 2021.

obligations.description: Services distributing youth-harmful online media must use age-gating or equivalent identity checks before allowing minors to access adult content
Youth-harmful media must be labelled and kept unavailable to minors

regulations.name: Utah Social Media Regulation Act (SB 152 & HB 311)
regulations.summary: Utah's 2023 social media laws targeted minor social media accounts through age verification, parental oversight, and restrictions on certain features and interactions. The regime has been heavily litigated and later revised, so these obligations should be treated as historically specific rather than a clean current-state template.

obligations.description: Social media companies were directed to verify whether a Utah user seeking an account was a minor or an adult
Minor social media accounts were tied to parental or guardian permission and oversight rights
Minor accounts were subject to design and access restrictions intended to limit addictive or unsafe use patterns
Minor accounts faced restrictions on communications and interactions with accounts outside the minor's connected network

milestones.description: SB 152 and HB 311 introduced in Utah Legislature
Passed Utah Legislature
Signed by Governor Cox
Laws took effect after delayed implementation

regulations.name: China Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) — Minor Protection Provisions
regulations.summary: China's comprehensive data protection law includes specific provisions for minors under 14, requiring parental consent and special data handling. Combined with gaming restrictions and real-name verification requirements.

obligations.description: Many covered services use real-name registration and related identity checks to distinguish minors and adults under Chinese online-service rules
Parental consent required for processing personal information of minors under 14
Gaming time restrictions for minors: 3 hours per week (Fri/Sat/Sun 8-9pm)
Platforms must implement special personal information processing rules for minors

milestones.description: Draft PIPL released for public comment
Passed by Standing Committee of NPC
PIPL came into effect
Enforcement began immediately with CAC oversight

regulations.name: Bill C-34, the Safe Social Media Act
regulations.summary: Canada introduced Bill C-34 on June 10, 2026. The bill would enact the Digital Safety Act and the Digital Safety Commission of Canada Act. It would regulate social media services and AI chatbot services through child-safety duties, digital safety plans, design obligations, complaint-handling tools, and an intended 16-year-old minimum age requirement for social media accounts, subject to commission exemptions for sufficiently safe services.

obligations.description: The government intends to impose a 16-year-old minimum age requirement for social media accounts, with possible commission exemptions for services that maintain sufficient safeguards for children
Regulated services would be subject to a core Duty to Protect Children, requiring safety-focused and age-appropriate design features
Regulated services would need to identify platform risks, adopt mitigation measures, make user guidelines available, and publish Digital Safety Plans

Social media services would be subject to duties to make certain content inaccessible and to act responsibly by mitigating harmful-content exposure, labelling synthetic content, and providing flagging and blocking tools
AI chatbot services would be required to act responsibly by mitigating harmful chatbot output, implementing emergency measures in crisis situations, and reducing harmful behaviour risks

milestones.description: Bill C-34, the Safe Social Media Act, was introduced
The earlier Online Harms Act (Bill C-63) lapsed when Parliament was prorogued

regulations.name: Indonesia Under-16 Social Media and High-Risk Platform Restrictions
regulations.summary: Indonesia began implementing restrictions in March 2026 that require social media and other designated high-risk digital platforms to block or deactivate under-16 accounts and use age-assurance measures chosen by the service.

obligations.description: Designated services must use age-assurance measures to identify users under 16
Platforms classified as harmful to children must block or suspend under-16 accounts
Platforms are expected to disclose the number of under-16 accounts they have suspended or closed

milestones.description: Indonesia began implementing the under-16 restrictions for designated digital platforms
Minister publicly pressed platforms to disclose compliance figures and account removals

regulations.name: Malaysia Under-16 Social Media Account Restrictions
regulations.summary: Malaysia began enforcing rules on June 1, 2026 that bar children under 16 from opening social media accounts on large platforms, require age verification, and phase verification for existing users.

obligations.description: Covered platforms must verify the age of new and existing users
Children under 16 cannot open accounts on covered social media services

milestones.description: Malaysia announced that under-16 social media restrictions would be rolled out nationally
Regulator began enforcing the under-16 account restrictions

regulations.name: Singapore Code of Practice for Online Safety
regulations.summary: Singapore's Code of Practice requires designated social media services to implement systems and processes that improve online safety for users, especially children. It is broader than age assurance and focuses on child-safety measures, reporting, and platform governance.

obligations.description: Designated social media services must provide safety features and account tools appropriate for child users
Platforms must implement measures to moderate and remove content harmful to children
Platforms must publish annual online safety reports including child safety metrics

milestones.description: IMDA launched public consultation on Code of Practice
Code of Practice issued by IMDA
Code came into effect for designated social media services
IMDA begins enforcement

regulations.name: EU AI Act — Child Safety and High-Risk AI Provisions
regulations.summary: The EU AI Act contains child-safety and risk-management provisions for certain AI systems, but it is not a general age-verification law for AI services. Obligations phase in over time based on system category.

obligations.description: Providers must consider foreseeable vulnerabilities of children when designing and managing relevant AI systems
Providers of high-risk AI systems must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments including impact on children

milestones.description: European Commission proposed the AI Act
Adopted by EU legislature
AI Act entered into force
